ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Japanese sausage curry rice. FOODON_00002809 (curry dish) used as closest parent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKW for Japanese-style curry used as closest parent; no specific sausage curry rice code exists in FoodEx2.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 450 g, comprising a generous portion of steamed white rice topped with Japanese curry sauce and three grilled or pan-fried pork sausages.

nutrient_source.title: Japanese curry rice with sausage — component-based estimation using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked white rice, Japanese curry sauce (roux-based), and pork sausage
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Japanese sausage curry rice as a composite dish. Values estimated from weighted component proportions: steamed white rice (~55% by weight, USDA SR Legacy #20045), Japanese curry roux sauce (~30% by weight, USDA Branded/SR Legacy analogue for curry sauce), and pork sausage (~15% by weight, USDA SR Legacy #10165). Proportions are consistent with standard Japanese curry rice restaurant servings. Energy and macros cross-checked against NCC Food and Nutrient Database category anchors for mixed rice dishes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — white rice and mixed rice-based dishes
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Japanese sausage curry rice. GI estimated at 60 based on the dominant carbohydrate component being steamed white rice (GI ~64–72 for Japanese short-grain rice per University of Sydney GI Database) moderated by the curry sauce (fat, protein, and fibre content lower the composite GI). The composite GI of 60 is consistent with published estimates for mixed rice-curry dishes in the medium GI range. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (60 × 94.5) / 100 = 56.7, where carbohydrate per 450 g serving = 21.0 × 4.5 = 94.5 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~380 mg per 100 g; ~1710 mg per serving) from curry roux and sausage
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its high sodium load (~1710 mg per serving), which represents approximately 74% of the WHO recommended daily sodium limit of 2300 mg; choosing a low-sodium curry roux and reducing sausage quantity can meaningfully lower sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load per serving (~56.7) driven by a large portion of white rice
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should be cautious with this dish as the high glycemic load (~56.7 per serving) from white rice can cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation; substituting brown rice or reducing the rice portion size and pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables can help moderate the glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat from pork sausage and curry roux, combined with high sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, the combination of saturated fat (~1.6 g per 100 g) from pork sausage and the high sodium content (~380 mg per 100 g) warrants portion control; selecting leaner protein alternatives and a lower-sodium curry preparation would reduce cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sausage Curry Rice (3 Pieces) is a Japanese-style comfort food dish consisting of steamed short-grain white rice served with a rich, mildly spiced Japanese curry sauce and three grilled or pan-fried pork sausages. Japanese curry (karē) is distinctively milder, sweeter, and thicker than South Asian curries, typically prepared from a roux-based curry block containing wheat flour, fat, and a blend of spices including turmeric, cumin, coriander, and fenugreek, along with simmered vegetables such as onion, potato, and carrot. The sausages add a savoury, slightly smoky protein element that complements the sweet-savoury curry sauce. The dish is a popular yoshoku (Western-influenced Japanese cuisine) staple widely served in Japanese curry restaurants and casual dining establishments across Japan and Singapore. Nutritionally, the dish is energy-dense (~148 kcal per 100 g; ~666 kcal per 450 g serving), with carbohydrates as the dominant macronutrient from white rice, moderate protein from sausage, and moderate fat from the curry roux and sausage. Sodium content is notably high (~380 mg per 100 g) due to the curry roux and processed sausage, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension. The glycemic load per serving is high (~56.7), which is clinically relevant for Type 2 Diabetes management. The dish contains gluten (from curry roux), soy, and potentially dairy, and is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Japanese sausage curry rice as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from weighted USDA SR Legacy component data for cooked white rice, Japanese curry sauce analogue, and pork sausage. GI is an analogous estimate based on the dominant carbohydrate component (white rice) moderated by the curry sauce composition. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component sources are well-established approved sources and the proportions are consistent with standard restaurant servings.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not reliably reported across the component sources used for this estimated composite dish. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is -1 as it is not consistently reported in the USDA SR Legacy entries used for the component estimation. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the primary component sources used. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, potassium, calcium, iron, cholesterol, vitamin C, vitamin A, B-vitamins, folate, phosphorus, magnesium, and zinc are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data and should be treated as approximate. The serving size of 450 g is an estimate based on standard Japanese curry rice restaurant portions; actual serving size at Maji Curry - Funan Mall may vary. Glycemic load per serving = (60 × 94.5) / 100 = 56.7, where 94.5 g carbohydrate = 21.0 g per 100 g × 4.5 (450 g serving). The dish is not confirmed halal due to the presence of pork sausage.
